coredata.dc:title: Impact on ratiometric fluorescence of carbon dots hybridizing with lanthanide in determination of residual Carbendazim in food
coredata.dc:creator: Han, Yong
Wang, Hui
Yu, Ying
Yang, Weixia
Shang, Fei
Li, Zhonghong
coredata.dc:description: Nano-hybrid carbon dots (CDs) with multiple emissions always endure fluorescence quenching induced by the interactions of chemical groups between hybrid CDs and nanomaterial, the samples with abundant interferences by an ordinary pretreatment. Lanthanide ion (DPA-Tb) was employed to hybridize PAN-CDs for the fabrication of ratiometric fluorescent sensor (PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb) to detect Carbendazim (MBC) in complicated food samples with facile pretreatment. The impacts of hybridization on the fluorescence of PAN-CDs were evaluated through dynamic characterizations, which manifested that DPA-Tb and the sensing matrix had no significant influence on the ratiomeric fluorescence of PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb sensor. The sensor (F363/F544) linearly responded to concentrations of MBC in 0–0.091 mmol/L with a limit of detection (LOD) of 3.0 × 10-3 μmol/L and satisfactory applications with recoveries of 92.22–106.08% and RSDs of 0.14–1.69%. The strategy provides a novel sight to investigate the fabrication of CDs-based ratiometric fluorescent sensors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Ratiometric fluorescence
Nanohybrid
Food safety
Pesticide
Carbendazim

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Impact on ratiometric fluorescence of hybrid CDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 32060571 ), CAS “Light of West China” Program (21JR7RM186), Longyuan Innovative Talents of Gansu, Gansu Education Department (2021A-123), and Qingyang Science and Technology Bureau (ZJ2021-16, QNKB2-4).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme illustration of PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb sensor toward MBC.
(A) TEM image of PAN-CDs, the inset was the particle distribution. (B) XRD pattern of PAN-CDs. (C) Absorbance and fluorescent spectra of PAN-CDs. (D) Excitation-independence fluorescence of PAN-CDs.

(A) FTIR spectra of PAN-CDs, MBC, and PAN-CDs + MBC. (B) FTIR spectra of DPA and DPA-Tb. (C) FTIR spectra of DPA-Tb, MBC, and DPA-Tb + MBC. (D) FTIR spectra of PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb, MBC and PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb + MBC.
(A) XPS survey spectrum of PAN-CDs. (B) High-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s from PAN-CD (C) High-resolution XPS spectrum of N1s from PAN-CD. (D) High-resolution XPS spectrum of O1s from PAN-CD.

(A) XPS survey spectrum of DPA-Tb. (B) High-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s from DPA-Tb. (C) High-resolution XPS spectrum of N1s from DPA-Tb. (D) High-resolution XPS spectrum of O1s from DPA-Tb. (E) High-resolution XPS spectrum of Tb3d from DPA-Tb.
(A) Fluorescent spectrum of PAN-CDs. (B) Fluorescent spectrum of DPA-Tb. (C) Fluorescent spectrum of PAN-CDs& DPA-Tb. (D) Absorbance and fluorescent spectra of PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb and MBC.
High-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s (A), N 1s (B), O 1s (C), and Tb 3d (D) from PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb.
Calibration line between fluorescent ratios of the sensor and MBC concentrations in the range of 0–0.091 mmol/L. (C) The selectivity of the sensor toward MBC.
Recovery test for the detection of MBC in real samples.

head.ce:title: Impact on ratiometric fluorescence of carbon dots hybridizing with lanthanide in determination of residual Carbendazim in food

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Agriculture and Forestry, Longdong University, Qingyang, Gansu 745000, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Agriculture and Forestry, Longdong University, Qingyang, Gansu 745000, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest A&F University, Yangling, Shaanxi 712100, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest A&F University, Yangling, Shaanxi 712100, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Gansu Key Laboratory of Protection and Utilization for Biological Resources and Ecological Restoration, Qingyang 745000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Gansu Key Laboratory of Protection and Utilization for Biological Resources and Ecological Restoration

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Gansu Key Laboratory of Protection and Utilization for Biological Resources and Ecological Restoration, Qingyang 745000, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: College of Agriculture and Forestry, Longdong University, Qingyang, Gansu 745000, PR China (Y. Han).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nano-hybrid carbon dots (CDs) with multiple emissions always endure fluorescence quenching induced by the interactions of chemical groups between hybrid CDs and nanomaterial, the samples with abundant interferences by an ordinary pretreatment. Lanthanide ion (DPA-Tb) was employed to hybridize PAN-CDs for the fabrication of ratiometric fluorescent sensor (PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb) to detect Carbendazim (MBC) in complicated food samples with facile pretreatment. The impacts of hybridization on the fluorescence of PAN-CDs were evaluated through dynamic characterizations, which manifested that DPA-Tb and the sensing matrix had no significant influence on the ratiomeric fluorescence of PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb sensor. The sensor (F363/F544) linearly responded to concentrations of MBC in 0–0.091 mmol/L with a limit of detection (LOD) of 3.0 × 10-3 μmol/L and satisfactory applications with recoveries of 92.22–106.08% and RSDs of 0.14–1.69%. The strategy provides a novel sight to investigate the fabrication of CDs-based ratiometric fluorescent sensors.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence spectrophotometry provides rapid measurements of diverse chemicals in different fields, especially in food safety monitoring, owing to the easy operation and facile readout in the procedure [1]. Recently, various nanomaterial-based fluorescent platforms have been developed for the detection of a wide variety of organic and inorganic targets, which provides an opportunity to address certain challenges of fluorescent detection. Among these nano-materials [2–4], fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) [5] is regarded as a desirable alternative in the fluorescent assay, which presents similar photo-physical performance and photochemical stability to the semi-conduct quantum dots (SCQDs). Moreover, it is feasible to adopt CDs into the sensing system of various analytes due to the non-blinking fluorescence emission and ready solubility with the major advantage of non-toxic elements with rare harm to the operator and environment [6]. There are increasing reports that focus on CDs sensors in rapid determination [7]. Nevertheless, much more endeavor is required to overcome these challenges of sensitivity and selectivity of fluorescent CDs sensors. Ratiometric fluorescent sensing with the virtue of self-calibration [8,9] is increasingly regarded as an efficient strategy for the CDs-based sensor to specifically recognize target analytes in a complicated matrix. Dual emissive CDs have been exploited as ratiometric fluorescent sensors to measure pH values, metal ions, and so on [10,11]. Nevertheless, dual emissive CDs are difficult to be routinely prepared through ligand functionalization. Nanohybrid CDs [12] fluorescent sensors have attracted increasing attention in the fabrication of CDs based ratiometric fluorescent sensors. The integration of nanomaterials and CDs has been wildly utilized in ratiometric fluorescent measurement in the past decade [13–17].

Despite the debated origin of fluorescence on CDs and the mechanisms of CDs photoluminescence are not yet fully understood, the application of CDs in fluorescent determination would have deserved an increase in a variety of fields [18]. However, as an organic QDs with abundant chemical groups around the carbon core are assumed that the quantum confinement effect and surface structure jointly govern fluorescent properties of CDs, the intrinsic emission of CDs can be facilely impacted by miscellaneous factors in a sensing system, such as anions and cations in the matrix, chemical groups from biomolecules, light and thermal energy from the environment. Nano-hybrid CDs with multiple emissions also suffer from the challenge of fluorescence quenching due to the interactions of chemical groups between CDs and nanomaterials. Moreover, for practical applications, there is an urgent need for a fluorescence detection method that can be applied through a simple pretreatment of samples, which means that a large number of interferents may be presented in this sensing system. Therefore, more and more studies are focusing on the effect of interferents on the fluorescence of nanohybrid CDs. Among the nanohybrid strategies, the lanthanide ions (Ln3+) integrated with CDs exhibits promising candidate for CDs based ratiometric fluorescent sensors [12] owing to the facile preparation, long-wave, and multiple emissions, and large Stock’s shift of fluorescent Ln3+. Generally, pyridine-2,6-dicarboxylic acid (DPA) is employed as an efficient sensitizer for Ln3+ via an “antenna effect” to extremely enhanced optical properties of Ln3+ to construct ratiometric fluorescent sensors [19,20]. In this case, the nanohybrid system arises abundant interferents, and the nanohybrid impact on the fluorescence of CDs in the ratiometric determination should be investigated for the extensive application of CDs-based fluorescent measurement.

Additionally, a new sensing system of CDs based ratiometric fluorescence should be validated by practical application, and the feasibility of food safety monitoring can be evaluated on the complicated food matrix. Agrochemicals have presented significant impacts on the quality of food, water, and the environment, especially food safety which has been a global concern [21–23]. Pesticides are considered as the most adverse health effects on people, which are strongly associated with food safety [24]. The small molecule pesticide Carbendazim (i.e. Methyl benzimidazole-2-ylcarbamate, MBC) is widely used all over the world, which poses the carcinogenic potential to the human being in long-term exposure [25]. Moreover, MBC has become the most frequently detected pesticide in the environment and agro-products [26]. Nowadays, chromatography has been frequently used to detect various pesticides. Unfortunately, the complicated sample pretreatment and time-consuming limit their practical applications in food analysis [27]. In terms of on-site and real-time, the rapid analysis of food safety in an intricate food matrix arouses much more attention to the time-lag effects of routine methods, which can avoid potential risks to food safety [28]. Therefore, the CDs-based ratiometric fluorescent measurement could be employed for the rapid and effective analyze MBC to ensure safe food [29]. Furthermore, the complex food matrix can be used to validate the practical application of CDs based ratiometric sensing system.

Herein, a selective ratiometric fluorescent sensor was handily fabricated by DPA sensitized terbium ion (Tb3+) integrating CDs for the sensitive detection of MBC in a food matrix. As illustrated in Fig. 1 , the as-synthesized blue-emissive PAN-CDs were mixed with green-emissive DPA-Tb in the proportion achieving the ratiometric fluorescent sensing system (PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb). Multiple characterizations were applied to investigate the impact of nanohybrid on the fluorescence of CDs. UV–Vis absorbance, fluorescence spectra, FTIR spectra and XPS characterization were employed to examine the effect on the fluorescence property of hybrid CDs during the integration of CDs and DPA-Tb, which was helpful for the construction of hybrid CDs sensor. Then the sensor was applied in the determination of MBC from a simple pretreatment food sample to further explore the feasibility and practical application of the sensor.

1-(2-Pyridylazo)-2-naphthol (PAN) (purity, 98%), 2, 6-Pyridinedicarboxylic acid (DPA), Terbium chloride hexahydrate (TbCl3·6H2O) were obtained from Aladdin Biotech Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Anhydrous magnesium sulfate (MgSO4) Sodium chloride (NaCl), Zinc sulfate (ZnSO4), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), Carbendazim (MBC) (purity, 99%), Thiophanate-methyl (TM) (purity, 99%), Chlorbenzuron (purity, 99%), Imidacloprid (purity, 99%), Acetic acid, Formic acid, Acetonitrile, Lysine (Lys), Vitamin C, Fructose and other interferents commercially available from Sinopharm chemical reagent Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). All the reagents used in the experiment were without further purification. A Milli-Q water purification system (Millipore, Bedford, MA, USA) was used to provide ultrapure water (UW). A dialysis bag (1000 Da) was obtained from Aladdin Biotech Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China), and a 0.22 μm PES filter membrane was purchased from Jingteng Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). A mixture of ethanol/methanol (v/v = 1:1) was applied to prepare stock solutions of MBC and TM within the concentration of 0.1 mmol/L, which were stored at 4 °C in glass vessels.

PAN-CDs were prepared by a one-pot hydrothermal approach using PAN as a precursor and ethanol as the solvent with certain modifications [30]. Briefly, 49.85 mg of PAN was dissolved in 20 mL ethanol with10 min ultrasonic treatment. The solution was heated hydrothermally in a Teflon-equipped stainless-steel auto-clave at 180 °C for 4 h, then centrifuged for 15 min at 8, 000 rpm to remove precipitates and filtered with a 0.22 µm PES membrane. The filtrate was dialyzed for 24 h in UW and collected for the following experiment. Secondly, DPA-Tb was prepared by using 0.0836 g of DPA dissolved in 50 mL of UW to obtain the stock solution of DPA with a concentration of 10 mmol/L. Then, the stock solution of DPA-Tb was obtained by dissolving 0.02 g TbCl3 in a 10 mL stock solution of DPA. Finally, the commixture of PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb was achieved by mixing PAN-CDs and DPA-Tb. Briefly, the stock solution of PAN-CDs was diluted by 2000 fold and the stock solution of DPA-Tb by 10 fold. Subsequently, the two diluted solutions were mixed in the proportion of 1:1 to obtain the composition for the following experiments.

The morphologies of the as-synthesized PAN-CDs were characterized by a transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEOL-S4800, FESEM, Japan) and a powder diffractometer (Bruker D8 Advanced Diffractometer System) with a Cu Ka (1.5418 Å) source to obtain a TEM image and an X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern. The photometric characteristics of the as-synthesized PAN-CDs were characterized by a spectrophotometer (UV–Vis 2500, Shimadzu, Japan) and a fluorescence spectrometer (LS-55, Perkin-Elmer, USA), and the fluorescent spectra were recorded by Fluorescence spectrometer (LS-55, Perkin-Elmer, USA) in the wavelength of at 300–600 nm at the excitation wavelength of at 280 nm with an excitation and emission slit widths of 5 nm. The composition and the chemical groups of PAN-CDs were examined through a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Vetex 70, Bruker Corp, Germany) and X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Axis Ultra DLD, Kratos, UK) equipped with an Al-Kα X-ray source (pass energy 100.0 eV, total acquisition time 1 min 8.0 secs, energy step size 1.000 eV). All samples were performed in triplicate in the measurements for statistical analysis.

Relied on the characterizations mentioned above, the fluorescence of as-synthesized PAN-CDs and DPA-Tb were separately optimized for the forthcoming integration. Firstly, a 10 mL diluted solution of PAN-CDs was prepared in a tube, then the diluted solution of DPA-Tb with different concentrations was separately dripped into the tube with certain CDs according to the preliminary experiments. The mixture solution of PAN-CDs and DPA-Tb was incubated at room temperature (about 20 ℃) over 10 min. Subsequently, the solution was characterized to explore the impact of DPA-Tb on the fluorescence of PAN-CDs. The same procedure was carried out to further explain the impact of analyte and food matrix on the fluorescent property of the optimized hybrid CDs-based sensor (PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb). According to the preliminary experiment, Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer solution (pH = 7.00, 10 mmol/L) was applied as the buffer system in the experiment, where 1 mL of PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb solution was dripped into 1 mL BR buffer and was incubated at room temperature for ∼2 min for the sensing system. Thereafter, 200 μL standard solutions of MBC with different concentrations were dripped into the system. After 5 min incubation at room temperature, the FL spectra were measured at the excitation wavelength of 280 nm with emission wavelengths of 363 and 544 nm. All fluorescence measurements of different solutions were performed in triplicate for the statistics.

Based on the linear relationship between the ratiometric fluorescence of the sensor and the concentrations of MBC, the calibration function (Eq. 2) of Fr (Eq. 1) against concentrations of MBC was observed, and the limit of detection (LOD) was estimated according to Eq. 3.
(1)
F r
=
F 363
+
F 544
(2)
F r
= α C + β
(3)
LOD = 3 σ / S
(3)
F
cor
F
obsd
=
2.3 d
A
ex
1 -
10
- d
A
ex
10
g
A
ex
2.3 s
A
em
1 -
10
- s
A
em

Where Fr means the ratio FL intensities of PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb sensor at 363 and 544 nm, C stands for the concentration of MBC, αand βare regarded as the slope and the intercept of the calibration function respectively, the standard deviation of blank signals of the PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb sensor (N = 10) is σ , and S is similar to α.

To evaluate the selectivity of the PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb sensor toward MBC, the general interferents coexisting with MBC in a matrix of fruit and vegetables were tested. Possible substances included positive charge ions, amino acids, saccharides, organic acids, as well as analogous pesticides. In this experiment, the sensor test Al3+, Na+, Hg2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, cysteine (Cys), lysine (Lys), phenylalanine (Phe), fructose, glucose, sucrose, citric acid, malic acid, tartaric acid, vitamin C, TM, and imidacloprid. In a typical test, 1 μmol/L of MBC was tested by the sensor, while 10 μmol/L of the analogous pesticides, and 100 μmol/L of the positive charge ions, amino acids, organic acids, and saccharides. All interferent was at least 10 folds concentration of MBC. Then the sensor worked in these solutions respectively to evaluate the selectivity of the sensor toward MBC under the same conditions mentioned in the calibration curve.

The MBC spiked spinach, cucumber, and pear were chosen to evaluate the feasibility and reliability of the PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb sensor in practical applications. A facile pretreatment was adopted to prepare the sample solutions. Briefly, standard solutions of MBC in a certain concentration were utilized to spray the surface of all the samples. Spiked samples were cut into pieces and homogenized with a mortar to obtain a mashed sample, and then a 5.0 g sample was transferred into a tube with 10 mL acetonitrile for 10 min. The insoluble impurities of the samples were removed by centrifugation at 8,000 rpm for 5 min, then the supernatant was treated with a water bath for 5 min at room temperature and filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane. Finally, the obtained solutions were transferred into 2 mL acetonitrile and were then diluted with a 10 mL mixture solution of ethanol/methanol (v:v = 1:1) to obtain the solutions for the following test.

The morphology of PAN-CDs presented in Fig. 2
A indicated the spherical particles of PAN-CDs ranged from 3.51 to 12.31 nm with a mean particle diameter of 7.40 nm statistically calculated by Nano Measurer 1.2 (Fudan University, China) (Fig. 2 A inset). The XRD spectrum (Fig. 2 B) with the main diffraction peak at ∼31.5° was identifiable to the previously reported work [16], which suggested the graphene-like pattern of PAN-CDs. The UV–Vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra of the as-prepared PAN-CDs exhibited in Fig. 2 C displayed three typical UV–Vis absorption peaks at 225 nm, 295 nm, and ∼350 nm, which corresponded to the n–σ* transitions (C-OH, C-NH) of the trapping of excited-state energy by the surface states of PAN-CDs, π-π* transitions (C=C) and n-π * transitions (C=O, C=N) of aromatic sp2 domains from PAN-CDs respectively [31]. Additionally, the fluorescent emission of the as-prepared PAN-CDs was at 363 nm when excited at 280 nm with excitation-independence fluorescence shown in Fig. 2 D. PAN-CDs were consistent with general CDs with carbon core and surface chemical groups [32].

FTIR spectra of PAN-CDs shown in Fig. 3
A exhibited the stretching vibration of the hydroxyl group from PAN-CDs resulting in a broad peak centered at ∼3450 cm−1. Peaks at 1384.89 and 1602.85 cm−1 were attributed to the symmetric and antisymmetric stretching vibrations of –COOH from the coordinated PAN-CDs in the composition [33]. A cluster of peaks in the range of 1300–1500 cm−1 in PAN-CDs referred to the structure of the benzene ring, especially the skeletal vibration of C=C in the benzene ring at the peak range from 1402 to 1622 cm−1. Peaks at 767.67 and 1074.35 nm of PAN-CDs were respectively ascribed to the wagging vibration of N-H and the bending vibration of the C-H in-plane of the benzene ring. Similarly, the chemical groups of DPA, DPA-Tb, and PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb were characterized by FTIR as shown in Fig. 3 B, C, and D. These above-mentioned observations manifested that all the chemicals maintained their typical structure during the integration and confirmed the residues of PAN and DPA in the nanohybrid PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb. These benzene groups could play a critical role in the recognition of MBC through π-π stacking [34]. Besides, the mixture of PAN-CDs and DPA-Tb could not induce a significant variation of each material, which claimed the weak interaction between PAN-CDs and DPA-Tb in the facile preparation of PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb.

The XPS spectra of PAN-CDs were analyzed to further explain the surface component and electronic structure of PAN-CDs shown in Fig. 4
A. The XPS survey spectrum displayed three characteristic peaks centered at ∼530, ∼400, ∼285, and ∼1242 eV, which respectively corresponded to O 1s, N 1s, and C 1s. Additionally, PAN-CDs presented negligible amounts of Cl, Na, F, and Si at peaks of 208.1,1076.7, 694.6, and 107.1 eV due to the pretreated test solution and the carrier material during the testing procedure, which can be ignored in compositions analysis for CDs generally. Despite, the survey spectrum presenting some undesired peaks, main compositions from PAN-CDs can be easily found with obvious peaks. The high-resolution spectrum of C 1s (Fig. 4 B) presented three different peaks at 288.6, 286.5, and 284.8 eV in PAN-CDs indicating the existence of different chemical states of carbon. The high-resolution spectrum of N 1s is depicted in Fig. 4 C and three peaks at 407.2, 400.2, and 399.1 eV in PAN-CDs were attributed to the bonds of NO3, graphitic N, and N-H respectively, indicating that most of the N is mainly at the surface forming part of the surface nitrogen-containing functionalities. The high-resolution spectrum of O 1s is depicted in Fig. 4 D and two peaks at 532.6 and 531.5 eV in PAN-CDs are attributed to the bonds of C-O, C=O, and the group of –COOH respectively [33].

The XPS survey spectrum of DPA-Tb (Fig. 5
A) displayed four characteristic peaks centered at ∼530, ∼400, ∼285, and ∼1242 eV, which respectively corresponded to O 1s, N 1s, C 1s and Tb 3d. The high-resolution spectrum of Tb 3d (Fig. 5 E) presented peaks at 1277.3 and 1242.3 eV in DPA-Tb respectively referred to as Tb 3d3/2 and Tb 3d5/2. Additionally, the high-resolution spectra of O 1s, N 1s, and C 1s from DPA-Tb were analyzed (Fig. 5 B, 5C, 5D) and these observations were approximately the same as their states of PAN-CDs. Nevertheless, the high-resolution peaks of the elements exhibited an obvious shift due to the electron shield effect from the interaction between DPA and Tb3+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact on ratiometric fluorescence of hybrid CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of hybrid CDs were displayed in Fig. 6 for the evaluation of the influence on the ratiometric fluorescence of hybrid CDs. The fluorescent spectra of PAN-CDs, DPA-Tb, and PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb were separately presented in Fig. 6 A, B, and C. The peak emission of PAN-CDs was at 363 nm, and the typical peak emissions of DPA-Tb were at 490 nm and 544 nm, which illustrated no interference between PAN-CDs and DPA-Tb on the fluorescence. The sensor PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb exhibited stable emissions at 363 nm, 490 nm and 544 nm when excited by 280 nm light shown in Fig. 6 D, which facilitated the preparation of the PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb sensor without interference between PAN-CDs and DPA-Tb. The absorbance spectrum of PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb displayed in Fig. 6 D consisted of the character of PAN-CDs and DPA-Tb, which further confirmed the slight influence on the fluorescence of PAN-CDs. Moreover, the chemical groups of DPA-Tb, PAN-CDs, and PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb were revealed by FTIR spectra referred to in Fig. 3. The stretching vibration of the hydroxyl group from DPA (Fig. 3 B) resulted in a broad peak centered at ∼3450 cm−1. Peaks at 1384.89 and 1602.85 cm−1 were attributed to the symmetric and antisymmetric stretching vibrations of –COOH from the coordinated DPA in the composition. A cluster of peaks in the range of 1300–1500 cm−1 in DPA-Tb (Fig. 3 C) referred to the structure of the benzene ring, especially the skeletal vibration of C=C in the benzene ring at the peak range from 1402 to 1622 cm−1. Peaks at 767.67 and 1074.35 nm of PAN-CDs were respectively ascribed to the wagging vibration of N-H and the bending vibration of the C-H in-plane of the benzene ring. All these observations mentioned above confirmed the existence of residues of PAN and DPA in the PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb. These benzene groups can play a critical role in the recognition of MBC through π-π stacking. Besides, the mixture of PAN-CDs and DPA-Tb could not induce a significant variation of each material, which manifested the weak interaction between PAN-CDs and DPA-Tb in the facile preparation of PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb.

In addition, the XPS spectrum of PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb was applied to evaluate the influence on the ratiometric fluorescence of hybrid CDs and the structure mutation of PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb during the integration of PAN-CDs and DPA-Tb shown in Fig. S1 . The chemical composition of PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb was investigated and the characteristic peaks centered at ∼530, ∼400, ∼285 and ∼1242 eV in the XPS spectrum of PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb respectively corresponded to O 1s, N 1s, C 1s, and Tb 3d. The high-resolution deconvoluted spectra of C 1s (Fig. 7
A) presented three different peaks at 288.8, 285.7, and 284.8 eV in PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb indicating the existence of different chemical states of carbon, including –COOH/C=O, C-N, C-C/C=C of graphitic carbon. The high-resolution spectrum of N 1s is depicted in Fig. 7 B and three peaks at 402.1, 399.7, and 398.3 eV are attributed to the bonds of C-O, graphitic N, and N-H (amino) respectively. In the deconvoluted spectra of O 1s depicted in Fig. 7 C, the peaks at 533.1, 532.3, and 531.6 eV are assigned to the bonds of C-OH, O-C, and C=O/–COOH respectively [33]. The high-resolution spectra of Tb 3d (Fig. 7 D) presented three different peaks at 1277.4, and 1242.8 eV corresponding to Tb 3d3/2 and Tb 3d5/2. These observations confirmed the successful preparation of PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb with stable benzene rings and abundant chemical groups. Compared to PAN-CDs and DPA-Tb, the state of elements from PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb were maintained with slight interaction between groups on these materials, manifesting the stable structure of PAN-CDs and DPA-Tb without significant influence on the hybrid. These observations confirmed the successful preparation of PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb with stable benzene rings and abundant chemical groups. What is more, the sensor of PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb exhibited excitation-independence properties similar to PAN-CDs, which was depicted in Fig. S2 . Moreover, the composition of PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb/MBC was also analyzed by XPS measurement for the further explication of the analyte influence on the fluorescence of hybrid sensor of PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb in the following section.

Firstly, the experiments were optimized to observe the conditions for the construction of the sensing model on the basis of the hybrid PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb. The influence of pH on Fr of PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb and the MBC dripped PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb is demonstrated in Fig. S3
A. The Fr of PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb presents slight changes in the pH range from 3 to 7 with stable ratio fluorescence in the acidic condition. While the Fr increased by the ascending pH from 7 to 11 due to the deprotonation of PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb in the alkaline condition. A similar variation of fluorescence was observed during MBC dripped PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb. This supposed that the lower pH restricted the surface state of PAN-CDs and the fluorescent intensity at 363 nm decreased, which was unfavorable for trace detection of MBC due to the indisposed discrimination of intensities of PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb. The fluorescent intensity could be enhanced in a higher pH, but the deprotonation of MBC with disruption could lead to fake results by the sensor. Thus, the optimal system with pH 7.0 BR buffers was applied to detect MBC. The sensing system was also optimized on incubation time ( Fig. S3
B) and temperature ( Fig. S3
C), which demonstrated slight influences on the performance of the sensing system. The equilibrium of the system could rapidly achieve in 5 min after dripping MBC into the system. The incubation temperature posed a slight impact on the system and Fr remained stable from 15 to 40 ℃. Thus, the ambient temperature was available for sensing MBC in this system.

Subsequently, gradient MBC solutions were dripped into the sensing system of PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb to calibrate the model of MBC detection under the optimum condition of pH 7.0 in BR buffer solution according to the optimal experiment. The fluorescent spectra shown in Fig. S4
A illustrated that MBC could lead to the quenching of PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb observed at 363, 490, and 544 nm in accordance with the concentrations of MBC ranging from 0 to 0.091 mmol/L, and the calibration curve between Frs (F363/F544) and MBC was calculated as shown in Fig. 8
A, which exhibited a considerable linear relationship between Frs and MBC with a satisfactory correlation coefficient of 0.995. The estimated limit of detection (LOD) was 3.0 × 10-3 μmol/L, which was lower than the regulated limits (refer to Table S1) and the results of previous reports were shown in Table S2. Besides, the calibration curve between fluorescent ratios (F363/F490) and the concentrations of MBC in the range of 0–0.091 mmol/L was also calculated achieving a poor correlation coefficient of 0.852. Thus, the Fr (F363/F544) was chosen as the indicator of the sensing model.

Moreover, the coexisting substances of MBC were employed to evaluate the selectivity of the PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb sensor toward MBC shown in Fig. S4
B. The result presented that only MBC could induce the quenching of PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb both at 363 and 544 nm. Moreover, Fig. 8 B depicted prominent Fr for MBC among interference substances coexisting with MBC. MBC could achieve a higher Fr in the system, which was distinct from other coexisting substances. These results demonstrated the high selectivity of the PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb sensor toward MBC.

The recovery tests on the PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb sensor were performed to evaluate the feasibility and applicability of the sensor to detect MBC in real samples, including fruit (pear) and vegetables (cucumber and spinach). The tests obtained satisfactory results with recoveries ranging from 92.22% to 106.08% as illustrated in Table 1 . The relative standard deviations (RSDs) of the measurement were calculated lower than 1.69%. These results implied a satisfactory performance of the PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb sensor in MBC detection for practical application, which can be used for rapid detection of MBC for monitoring food safety.

Specific recognition of the sensor toward MBC in a complex food matrix was investigated to explain the sensing mechanism. A cluster of peaks in the range of 1300–1500 cm−1 attributed to the aromatic compounds was observed in the FTIR spectrum of PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb + MBC (refer to Fig. 3 D). The sharp peak at 1384.89 and 1602.85 cm−1 in the spectrum corresponded to the characteristic vibration of C=O from PAN and DPA, and the stretching vibrations of C-N could be recorded as the sharp peak at 1087.86 cm−1. Similar to PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb, the stretch vibration of O-H in MBC was observed as the broad peak centered at ∼3450 cm−1, and the stretch vibration of N-H in MBC could be read as the peak at 3320 cm−1. These results implied that the benzene rings from PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb were maintained in the composition, which could recognize MBC through π-π stacking based on the benzene rings. Subsequently, the interactions among the active groups, such as –NH- on MBC and –COOH on PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb, resulted in fluorescent quenching, relying on photoinduced electron transfer (PET) and decrease of the surface state of PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb. Furthermore, the Tb3+ referenced CDs and the interactions between PAN-CDs, DPA-Tb and MBC were characterized by FTIR (Fig. 3 A and C). The chemical composition of PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb/MBC was investigated by XPS, and the results referred to Fig. S1 , where the characteristic peaks centered at ∼530, ∼400, ∼285 and ∼1242 eV in the XPS spectrum of PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb/MBC respectively corresponded to O 1s, N 1s, C 1s, and Tb 3d. The high-resolution spectrum of N1s of PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb/MBC shown in Fig. S5
B demonstrated that peaks at 399.62 and 399.34 eV could be ascribed as the bond of N-O, pyrrolic N, and N-H respectively, and the peak at 407.3 eV disappeared. Compared to PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb, the binding energy of these bonds in PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb/MBC exhibited a slight decrease, implying that the π-π stacking between PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb and MBC could increase the electron density of the aromatic rings enhancing the electron shielding to lower the binding energy of the bonds in and around the aromatic rings. Similar to the state of N 1s, the high-resolution spectrum of O 1s in PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb/MBC (refer to Fig. S5
C) demonstrates the peaks at 532.76, 531.84, and 531.05 eV representing the bonds of C-O, O-C, and C=O/NO3 respectively. Compared to PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb, the binding energy of C-O and C=O/NO3 presented a slight decrease, which supposed that the π-π stacking between PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb and MBC could increase the electron density of the aromatic rings enhancing the electron shielding to lower the binding energy of the bonds in and around the aromatic rings. The high-resolution spectra of C 1s and Tb 3d were analyzed (referred to as Fig. S5
A and S5D) and there was no significant variation observed, which was speculated that no covalent interaction took place between MBC and PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb in consist with the π-π stacking. As aforementioned, the peak at 407.3 eV in the high-resolution spectrum of N 1s from PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb disappeared after adding MBC, which implied that the electron donator group of –NH3+- had donated electrons to the electron acceptor groups of –COOH and –OH. The electron transfer resulted in decreasing binding energy of the electron acceptor bonds, such as the bond of C-O at the peak of 533.08 eV and the bond of C=O at the peak of 531.36 eV. These results further manifested that aromatic rings of MBC and PAN and DPA residues on PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb enabled the π-π stacking between MBC and PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb and improved the interaction between –COOH and –NH- from PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb and MBC respectively, resulting in the PET with fluorescent quenching eventually. The π-π stacking endowed the sensor with high selectivity to MBC, and MBC could continuously quench the sensor with a stable and continuous ratio of fluorescence. Commonly, the change of surface traps or electron-hole radiative recombination of CDs via Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) mode could result in effective fluorescent quenching [35,36]. Characterizations of the inner filter effect (IFE) [3] were conducted and the results can be found in the supporting information (refer to Table S3). According to these data, we can infer that the fluorescent quenching of the sensor was associated with IFE which enhanced the sensitivity of the sensor to MBC. There are several important differences between FRET and IFE. Generally, the excitation energy has been absorbed by the sensor in this work inducing the electron transfer between the donor and acceptor in FRET. However, the excitation energy was absorbed/blocked by MBC via IFE leading to the quenching of the sensor. Synergistic effects of PET and IFE enhanced the sensitivity of the sensor toward MBC. Besides, the synchronous response of dual-emission fluorescence advanced the selectivity and sensitivity of the sensor toward MBC, which was most helpful in selectively recognizing MBC in real samples.

Simultaneously, the PET is related to the FRET [1] resulting in the fluorescent quenching. As aforementioned, the IFE might be another dominant factor in quenching PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb. The IFE in this system was estimated according to eq.4. The result shown in Fig. S4
C manifested the existence of IFE in the system.
(4)
F
cor
F
obsd
=
2.3 d A
ex
1 -
10
- d
A
ex
10
g
A
ex
2.3 s
A
em
1 -
10
- s
A
em

Where Fobsd is the observed intensity, Fcor is the corrected intensity, and Aex and Aem are the absorbance per centimeter at the excitation and emission wavelengths, respectively. The remaining terms, d, g, and s, depend upon the geometry of the measurement and are defined in Fig. S4
C inset.

In summary, the ratiometric fluorescent sensor of PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb was facilely developed through hybrid CDs with lanthanide ions for the sensitive detection of pesticide MBC in a simple pretreated food matrix. Despite multiple factors being investigated in the sensing system, no significant impacts on the fluorescence of hybrid sensor of PAN-CDs&DPA-Tb. The π-π stacking was the main factor in the sensor for the selective recognition of MBC based on the residues of PAN and DPA from the sensor. The innate fluorescence of MBC and the high absorbance in the short wavelength range acted as a crucial role in the determination of MBC based on fluorescent quenching induced by IFE. Additionally, the PET induced fluorescent quenching was another main factor for the selective recognition of the sensor to MBC. Coupling these two factors, the sensor demonstrated excellent sensitivity and selectivity to MBC, and the radiometric fluorescence endowed a favorable response to detecting MBC to avoid most interference in the food matrix. In this scenario, the sensor preserved a linear range of 0–0.091 mmol/L MBC with a lower LOD of 3.0 × 10-3 μmol/L providing a higher sensitivity toward MBC. Furthermore, the sensor demonstrated an excellent selectivity to MBC against the potential interference substances in the sample and a feasible application in real samples with the recovery of 92.22–106.08% and RSDs of lower than 1.69%, which promises a potential alternative in the rapid determination of MBC residues in food samples. The strategy of nano-hybridizing CDs advanced the facile and rapid construction of ratiometric fluorescent sensors. Whereas, various nanomaterials deserved to be taken into account in the hybrid CDs, rather than only lanthanide ions, for the novel ratiometric fluorescent sensors in the future measurement.

Yong Han: Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Writing – original draft. Hui Wang: Funding acquisition, Methodology, Investigation. Ying Yu: Data curation, Formal analysis, Software. Weixia Yang: Formal analysis, Software. Fei Shang: Formal analysis, Software. Zhonghong Li: Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 32060571), CAS “Light of West China” Program (21JR7RM186), Longyuan Innovative Talents of Gansu, Gansu Education Department (2021A-123), and Qingyang Science and Technology Bureau (ZJ2021-16, QNKB2-4).

The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
